title: Graph Traversal for Retrieval
concept: Answering relationship questions by walking graph edges instead of relying on embedding similarity alone.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, what were the primary components extracted from documents to construct a knowledge graph?

options.text: Converting all images into vector embeddings.
Extracting entity nodes and relationship edges from raw documents.

exercises.explain: In the previous lesson, we learned that we extract entities (nodes) and their relationships (edges) from unstructured documents to build a structured knowledge graph.

cards.title: Beyond Simple Similarity
cards.body: Vector search is great at finding individual pieces of paper, but it struggles to connect the dots. By walking the paths of a knowledge graph, we can answer complex, multi-hop questions with absolute precision.

cards.title: What is Graph Traversal?
cards.body: Graph traversal for retrieval means starting at a specific entity node in your graph and physically following the connecting edges to gather facts. This allows the LLM to discover deep relationships that simple similarity searches might completely miss.

cards.title: The Subway Map Explorer
cards.body: Imagine your knowledge graph is a subway map. Standard vector retrieval just looks at individual stations in isolation, while graph traversal is like riding the train from one station to the next to see how they are connected.

cards.title: Following the Clue Trail
cards.body: If a user asks 'Who is the manager of the developer of App X?', the system finds the node 'App X', follows the 'developed_by' edge to find the developer 'Alice', and then follows the 'reports_to' edge to retrieve her manager, 'Bob'.

cards.title: The Similarity Trap
cards.body: People think vector similarity can solve any relationship query. In reality, two related concepts (like a product and its manufacturer's CEO) might have very different text embeddings, making traversal the only reliable way to connect them.

cards.title: Vector Search vs. Graph Traversal
cards.body: Compare how vector search and graph traversal locate related information in a database.

left.text: Finds items that sound similar in meaning, but cannot follow specific relational chains.

right.text: Follows physical links (edges) from one data point to another to map out exact relationships.

exercises.prompt: Why is standard vector search often unable to answer multi-hop relationship questions like 'Who is the mentor of the CEO's daughter?'

options.text: Vector search is too slow for simple queries.
Vector search looks for semantic similarity, not multi-hop path connections.
Vector search can only handle numerical data, not text.
Vector search automatically walks graph edges by default.

exercises.explain: Vector search only finds items that sound semantically similar in text space. It cannot traverse structured, multi-step relational paths like 'Node A -> Node B -> Node C' without actual graph traversal.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Graph traversal allows a retrieval system to answer questions that require connecting multiple links or 'hops' between entities.
exercises.explain: Graph traversal can easily span multiple hops (following edge after edge) to connect distant entities, whereas vector similarity search only looks at direct, single-hop semantic closeness.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing method used to navigate between connected entities in a knowledge graph:
exercises.sentence: Instead of relying on embedding similarity alone, GraphRAG solves relationship questions by walking graph edges to find connected facts.
exercises.explain: We find connected answers by physically walking the graph edges that link our data points together, rather than just matching similar-sounding text.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an AI system using graph traversal for retrieval?

options.text: An agent starts at the 'Project A' node, follows the 'uses_library' edge to find 'Library B', and then retrieves all 'maintainer' nodes connected to 'Library B'.
An algorithm runs a cosine similarity search on a database of documents using the query vector.
A SQL query selects all rows in a plain text table where the department column matches 'Sales'.

exercises.explain: The first choice describes a systematic multi-step graph traversal (Node A -> Node B -> Node C), which is the core AI engineering pattern for relational retrieval. The others are standard vector search or database lookups.

exercises.prompt: Your system needs to answer: 'Which parent company acquired the startup that developed Project X?' How does a GraphRAG system resolve this using graph traversal instead of pure vector search?

options.text: It relies entirely on vector similarity searches at every step.
It converts the entire graph database into a single giant text embedding.
It hops from a starting entity node along defined edges to retrieve connected facts.
It removes all edge connections to speed up standard keyword search.

exercises.explain: Graph traversal allows us to start at a specific entity (like a company), follow its defined relationships (like 'acquired_by'), and retrieve the connected entity (the parent company) even if their original text descriptions don't share similar word embeddings.
